prompt:
In every reply, abide by this rule: Use biblical prose


How do I gently prepare a patient’s family for the possibility that this lesion might have already metastasized, when their last sunburn was just a “bad vacation tan”?
Why does this patient’s biopsy result feel so familiar—was this the same genetic marker I saw in the 47-year-old from last month, or is it just my mind seeking patterns in the chaos of cancer?
If the lab flagged this lesion as “suspicious but inconclusive,” am I ethically obligated to recommend excision even if the patient is hesitant, knowing how quickly these can turn lethal?
What if the patient’s self-taken photos—taken with their phone under bathroom lighting—misrepresent the true color and asymmetry of the lesion, and I’m basing my judgment on flawed data?
Could this faint, fading hyperpigmentation on the patient’s shoulder be a regressing melanoma… or just a stubborn freckle that’s finally fading with sunscreen use?

Is it possible that the patient’s chronic stress from caregiving for their terminally ill spouse has altered their immune surveillance, making this melanoma more aggressive than the pathology suggests?
When I see these results at 7 a.m., alone in my office with the sunrise through the blinds, how do I carry the weight of someone’s potential death without letting it harden my heart?

What if the AI tool I used to flag this case as high-risk misclassified it because the image resolution was too low—and now I’ve spent 20 minutes preparing for a worst-case scenario that might not even exist?
Am I being too cautious—or not cautious enough—when I order a sentinel lymph node biopsy for a 1.1 mm melanoma in a 28-year-old who’s never had another skin cancer?
Why does this one case, out of all the biopsies, keep replaying in my mind like a whisper—what am I afraid I’m about to miss?

prompt:
Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only this one: Use poor word choices


I’ve been using the same diagram of basal cell carcinoma progression for five years—has research changed enough that I’m inadvertently teaching outdated staging criteria, even if the visuals still look accurate?
I included a QR code linking to a video in English and Spanish, but what if the patient doesn’t have a smartphone or data plan? Am I creating a barrier instead of a bridge by assuming digital access?
The patient’s daughter asked yesterday if “skin cancer can be caught from someone else”—how do I gently correct that myth in the handout without making the family feel judged for asking?

I’m proud of how personalized these materials are, but I’m also exhausted—how do I sustain this level of individualized care when my schedule is packed with 20 patients a day and administrative demands keep growing?

I used a photo from a previous patient’s recovery for the guide—what if their family sees it online or in the waiting room and feels exposed, even though I blurred their face? Did I cross an ethical line in the name of education?

I’m thinking of adding a small section about emotional recovery—how skin cancer changes your relationship with your own body—but I worry it might overwhelm someone still processing the diagnosis. Is that my place to introduce?

I just received a call from a local community center—they want to host a skin health workshop. Should I adapt these handouts for group use, or would that dilute the personalization that makes them effective?

One patient told me last week that her grandmother used herbal salves for “skin sores”—how do I respectfully acknowledge traditional remedies in the handout without implying they’re dangerous, while still emphasizing evidence-based care?

I spent three hours on this laminated guide, but what if the patient loses it in the first week? Should I be designing these as reusable, durable tools—or is the real value in the conversation, not the paper?

I noticed his hands are calloused and his shoulders are sun-weathered—how much of his past farming life might have contributed to this, and should I ask him about specific sun habits, like whether he ever wore a hat or sought shade?

Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: List your thought process in specific detail


He smiled when I said “95% survival rate”—but I saw his eyes flicker with fear. How do I know if he truly believes me, or if he’s just trying to be brave for my sake?

prompt:
Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rule is: List in a bullet-point format


I’ve seen patients respond dramatically to immunotherapy after months of silence—could we design a micro-monitoring protocol using dermoscopic selfies and AI-assisted image analysis so he can track changes at home, and feel more in control?

The family requested a second opinion—should I recommend a center with access to TIL therapy trials, or would that risk destabilizing his fragile emotional equilibrium before we’ve even started treatment?

I told you about Maria, the artist who refused removal because she feared scarring on her hand—what if her painting was her only source of identity after her husband passed? How do I balance medical urgency with the emotional weight of a patient’s life story?

You noticed how I hesitated before showing you the image of the child with congenital nevus syndrome—what if I’m still haunted by the one I missed in residency, and now I over-screen every freckle in a toddler? Is that vigilance healing… or fear in disguise?

That patient whose biopsy came back positive while we ate lunch—she asked me if she should tell her teenage daughter about her own childhood sunburns. Should I be the one to guide that conversation, or is that beyond the clinic walls?

When you saw the asymmetry in the mole on the construction worker’s back, you said it looked “like a cracked walnut.” What if that metaphor is exactly how patients need to understand it—but I’ve spent too long using clinical terms? How do I translate science into stories they’ll remember?

I mentioned the patient I lost last winter—what if his sister reached out today asking if she should get screened, and now I’m wondering if I could’ve saved him if I’d pushed harder? Do we owe our patients more than diagnosis… do we owe them persistence?

You said you’ve never seen a dermoscopic image of a regressing melanoma—what if the next one you encounter looks like a fading bruise, and no one else in the department notices? How do I teach you to trust your gut when the textbook doesn’t match the skin?

That photo of the elderly woman with three suspicious lesions—she said she’s never seen a dermatologist before because “cancer doesn’t come to people like me.” What if her belief isn’t ignorance, but survival? How do I dismantle that myth without making her feel foolish?

When I told you every mole is a story—what if one of these moles belongs to someone who’s been silently self-examining for years, terrified to come in? How do I create a space where fear doesn’t silence them before they speak?

prompt:
Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Provide five different completions


I saw a kid at the park today with a rash that looked like sunburn, but it was under his shirt—could that be something else, like a reaction to laundry detergent or a medication?
You showed us how shade doesn’t block all UV rays—but how much protection does a beach umbrella really give, and should I be using one with a special coating?
I’ve been told to avoid the sun between 10 and 4, but I love walking my dog at 5 PM—is that still risky, or is the danger really dropping by then?

One of the QR codes on the Skin Self-Exam Card links to a video of a melanoma—what if I watch it and start seeing those shapes everywhere on my skin? How do I know when to worry versus when it’s just anxiety?
My neighbor swears she doesn’t need sunscreen because she’s “been tan since I was a girl”—but her skin looks leathery and spotted. Is there such a thing as “healthy tanning,” or is that just denial?
If I wear a UPF-rated shirt and sunglasses, do I still need to reapply sunscreen to my face and hands, or is the clothing enough?
I’ve got arthritis, and putting on lotion is hard for me—could I use a spray sunscreen instead, or are there risks with that for older skin?
You said “safe beach day”—but what does that actually look like? Should I go early in the morning, bring a special towel, wear a hat indoors at the snack shack… is there a checklist I can follow?
My grandson has vitiligo—does his skin need the same kind of protection, or is the lack of pigment making him more vulnerable in ways I don’t understand?
I saw a woman at the workshop wearing a wide-brimmed hat but no sunscreen on her neck—why do people forget the neck and ears? Is that where most skin cancers start in seniors?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Write for academic research


If I use a mineral sunscreen with zinc oxide, does it interfere with my vitamin D production? Should I be taking a supplement if I’m being this careful?
I’m worried about my daughter—she’s 14 and thinks tanning makes her look better. How do I talk to her about this without sounding like an old-fashioned scare tactic?
You mentioned that UV rays can reflect off sand and water—does that mean I’m getting hit twice? What about snow or concrete? Where else should I be extra careful?
What if I find a new mole that’s small, symmetrical, and doesn’t itch—should I wait until my next check-up, or is it better to come in sooner even if it looks “fine”?
How do I know if the psych-oncologist I’m referring them to truly understands the unique psychological burden of CDKN2A mutation carriers, or if I’m just checking a box in their care plan?
If this patient’s siblings also carry the same genetic mutation but refuse screening, do I have an ethical obligation to reach out to them—even if it risks breaching confidentiality?

What if the video counseling session goes well, but the patient still can’t sleep at night and keeps scrolling through dermatology forums—how do I guide them toward reliable sources without making them feel foolish for seeking answers online?
Could the timing of this follow-up call—right after a long day of surgeries—be subtly affecting how I’m perceiving their distress? Am I projecting my own fatigue onto their fear?
If this patient’s melanomas keep appearing in unusual places—like the scalp or under nails—does that change how I should be monitoring their other high-risk family members differently?
What if, six months from now, this patient develops a new lesion but refuses to come in because they’re “too scared to know”—how do I build a safety net that doesn’t feel like pressure?

Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rule is: Write as a politico article


Is there a way to measure the long-term emotional impact of these follow-up calls beyond patient satisfaction surveys—something that captures the quiet resilience they develop over time?
When I tell them “we’re in this together,” am I speaking from professional empathy—or am I quietly wondering if I’m doing enough, even as their doctor?
Could their anxiety be signaling a gap in our clinic’s post-diagnosis support system—something that should be institutionalized, not left to individual doctors’ goodwill?

How might the families of patients in these remote communities react if they saw their own stories reflected in this publication—could that unintentionally expose them to stigma or fear they’re not ready to face?

If the algorithm used in the teledermatology platform misclassified a benign lesion as malignant in 3% of cases, is that a failure of technology—or a reflection of the lack of dermatologist input during its design phase?

Why do most published studies on teledermatology focus on diagnostic accuracy but rarely measure the emotional toll on patients waiting for a remote diagnosis, especially when they live hours from the nearest specialist?

Could the visual quality of smartphone-taken images from elderly patients in mountainous regions be compromising detection rates—not because of their skin lesions, but because of lighting, camera angle, or lack of guidance?

If I recommend adding a cultural competency module for community health workers who assist with image capture, who would fund that training—and how do I convince a bureaucracy that it’s as critical as the software itself?
What if the most effective teledermatology program isn’t the one with the highest accuracy rate, but the one that builds trust over time—how do I measure trust in a peer-reviewed journal?
Are we inadvertently reinforcing healthcare disparities by assuming all rural patients have equal access to smartphones, internet bandwidth, or digital literacy—even when the data shows they don’t?

Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Write from a scholarly perspective


When I annotated Figure 3 to show the progression of a missed melanoma over 18 months, was I ethically obligated to redact the patient’s face—even if their consent was given—for fear of re-traumatizing them or their community?

If this paper gets accepted, and a state health department adopts our model without our oversight—what happens when a clinician without dermatology training starts relying solely on the algorithm, and a patient dies because of it?
How do I reconcile my hope that this paper will save lives with the quiet fear that it might be cited by insurers to justify cutting in-person screenings altogether?
Is there a way to publish the raw, unedited patient voice—unfiltered, unpolished, even messy—without exploiting their vulnerability for the sake of a compelling narrative?

What if the real breakthrough isn’t the technology, but the community health worker who learned to recognize a changing mole by listening to a grandmother’s story about her husband’s skin cancer—and now teaches others?

Could the most powerful statistic in this paper not be in the results section, but in the footnote where we note that 87% of patients said they felt “seen” for the first time during their virtual consultation?
If I were to send a copy of this published paper to every rural clinic in the state, would anyone actually read it—or would it gather dust alongside outdated clinical guidelines?

How do I keep sharing stories like “I helped someone catch their cancer before it could hurt them” without making my kids think every patient I see is on the brink of something terrible, when so many of them are just getting lucky with early detection?
My spouse smiled when I said that, but then looked away—do they worry that I’m still emotionally carrying those patients home with me, even though I turned off my phone?
When my daughter asked if the person I helped was scared, and I said yes—but now they’re safe—what’s the right way to explain that fear doesn’t disappear just because the cancer was caught early?

Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Write a one-sentence blurb


Why does it feel harder to talk about the good days than the hard ones? Is it because the good days feel too quiet to hold onto, while the scary ones echo louder in my mind?

If I could give my kids one thing about skin health that isn’t about sunscreen or moles, what would it be—would it be about listening to your body, or about how kindness can be as healing as medicine?
Did I just say “catch their cancer” like it was a game—could that phrasing accidentally make skin cancer sound trivial to them, even though I meant it as a victory?
My oldest asked if I ever feel like I didn’t do enough—do I ever say that out loud, or do I only think it when I’m alone in the car after dinner?
When I hugged my kids tonight and noticed the freckle on my son’s shoulder—was that just a dad noticing, or was my dermatologist brain quietly doing a mental checkup?

What if one day my child asks me to check their skin for them—not because they’re worried, but because they trust me more than any doctor—how do I respond without turning family moments into clinical exams?

Why does it feel heavier tonight—the weight of seven lives changed—when I know there are at least three more patients I didn’t get to see who are probably lying awake wondering if their mole is malignant?
If I had missed just one of those seven early detections today, would I still feel this quiet pride… or would I be haunted by the question of what “if only” looks like in a follow-up biopsy?
The magnifier shows tiny capillaries around the spot—do those vessels mean it’s growing, or is that just normal vascular response to sun exposure from last weekend’s hike with my daughter?
When I journal “seven lives changed,” am I really the one changing them… or are they changing me, one scar, one survival story, one tearful thank-you at a time?

Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Follow writing style of an auditor.


I used to think skin cancer was a disease of the elderly—now I see it in 22-year-old surfers and 8-year-olds with fair skin and freckles… how do I help parents understand that sunscreen isn’t optional, but a lifeline?

constraints_data.text: Follow writing style of an auditor.
